The paper is organized as follows. In the first section, we introduce or develop the

For the rest of the paper, we write G = SL(2, R), K = SO(2), U = SU(2), unless
it is explicitly stated otherwise. We denote by g, k, u the associated Lie algebras.
Moreover, for any real Lie algebra a, the associated enveloping ∗-algebra U(a) is
defined as the complexification of its universal enveloping algebra, endowed the
unique ∗-structure such that the elements of a are skew-adjoint.

1. Operator algebraic preliminaries The goal of this first section is to introduce some background material that will
facilitate the construction of the quantum Mackey deformation field. For the rest
of the paper, the space of bounded operators and the space of compact operators
over any Hilbert space H are denoted by B(H) and K(H), respectively.

1.1. Continuous fields of Hilbert spaces and C*-algebras. Throughout the
paper, several continuous fields of Banach spaces will be introduced. Here we recall
some of their aspects and fix notations, referring to [17, Chapter 10] for a complete
exposition of the topic. More importantly, we introduce a notion of constraint on the
continuous fields of elementary C*-algebras associated to locally trivial continuous
field of Hilbert spaces. In the following, X denotes any locally compact space.
Let B be any continuous field of Banach spaces over X. The fiber of B over
each x ∈X is denoted by Bx and more generally, for any locally compact subspace
Y ⊂X, the continuous field over Y induced by B is denoted by B|Y . The space
of continuous vector fields of B is denoted by Γ(B) and its subspace consisting of
vector fields vanishing at infinity is denoted by Γ0(B). For any b ∈Γ(B), we write
bx for the evaluation of b at any x ∈X.
Let B′ be another continuous field of Banach spaces over X. By definition, an
embedding of continuous fields T : B →B′ is a collection of isometries (Tx : Bx →
B′
x)x∈X mapping Γ(B) into Γ(B′). Such an embedding of continuous fields T is
called an isomorphism if Tx is surjective for all x ∈X. In that case, [17, §10.2.4]
shows that Γ(B) is mapped onto Γ(B′). When B′ is a constant field, an isomorphism
T : B →B′ is referred to as a trivialization of B
We call B a continuous subfield of B′ if Bx is a Banach subspace of B′
x for all
x ∈X and if Γ(B) ⊂Γ(B′). We shall use repeatedly the two following facts.

• Given a Banach subspace Sx of Bx for each x ∈X, there is at most one
continuous subfield of B whose fibers are the Sx. Such a continuous subfield
exists if and only if for any x ∈X and any v ∈Bx, there exists b ∈B such
that bx = v and bx′ ∈Sx′ for all x′ ∈X.
• If Y is a closed subset of X, then the restriction map Γ0(B) →Γ0(B|Y ) is
surjective.

For the first fact, the uniqueness follows from [17, §10.2.4].
The second fact is
obtained by combining [17, §10.1.12] and [17, §10.2.6].
We call B a continuous field of Hilbert spaces if each of its fibers is a Hilbert
space. In that case, by polarization, the pointwise inner product of two continuous
vector fields is continuous over X.
We call B a continuous field of C*-algebras if each of its fibers is a C*-algebra and
if Γ(B) is stable by pointwise multiplication and involution. In that case, Γ0(B) has
a natural C*-algebra structure. Any of its irreducible representations is of the form
π ◦evx : Γ0(B) ↠Bx →B(H), where evx denotes the evaluation at some point
x ∈X and (π, H) is an irreducible representation of Bx. In that way, the spectrum
of Γ0(B) identifies with the disjoint union of the spectra of Bx for all x ∈X. A
continuous subfield of C*-algebras of B is defined as a continuous subfield whose
space of continuous vector fields is a ∗-subalgebra of Γ(B) (this implies that the
fibers are C*-subalgebras). An embedding of continuous fields of C*-algebras is an
embedding of continuous fields which is a fiberwise C*-algebra morphism.

Convention. It is a well known fact that a continuous field of C*-algebras B is
uniquely characterized by the C*-algebra Γ0(B) and the embedding of C0(X) into
its central multiplier algebra. For that reason, by abuse of notation, the C*-algebra
Γ0(B) will also be denoted by B.

convenience. Theorem 1.5. Let φ : A →B be a morphism of C*-algebras, with B postliminal.
Assume that (i) for every irreducible representation π of B, the pullback representation φ∗π
of A is irreducible,
(ii) the induced map φ∗: ˆB →ˆA between the spectra of both algebras is bijective. Then φ is an isomorphism. This version is a combination of [17, §2.7.3] and [17, §11.1.6]. In the following, we
actually use this theorem only for liminal C*-algebras [17, §4.2.1]. By definition,

of the representation theory of G. Since this is discussed in many textbooks, for

The Hecke algebra of the pair (g, K), defined in [23, §I.3], is denoted by R(g, K).
It is the convolution algebra of K-finite distributions on G supported in K. As
such, it is naturally endowed with a ∗-structure. The category of non-degenerate
(or approximately unital) R(g, K)-modules corresponds to the category of (g, K)-
modules, see [23, Theorem 1.117]. Accordingly, a (g, K)-module is said to be unitary
or unitarizable if the associated R(g, K)-module is.
The unitary representation theory of G essentially reduces to the study of the
(g, K)-modules. As an illustration of this principle, there is a one-one correspon-
dence between irreducible unitary representations of G and unitarizable simple
(g, K)-modules. It is defined as follows: if V is a simple unitary (g, K)-module
then its Hilbert completion ¯V is an irreducible representation of G and V corre-
sponds to the space K-finite vectors of ¯V , see [6, §3].
The main way to produce interesting representations of G is parabolic induction.
